title: Json
description: The #[Json] attribute marks an action as a JSON endpoint, returning data directly to JavaScript. Validation errors trigger a promise rejection with structured error data. This is ideal for actions consumed by JavaScript rather than rendered in Blade.

sections.title: #Basic usage
sections.content:
Apply the #[Json] attribute to any action method that returns data for JavaScript consumption:
The search() method returns posts directly to Alpine, where they're stored in the posts array and rendered client-side.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/⚡search.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\Json;use Livewire\Component;use App\Models\Post; new class extends Component { #[Json] public function search($query) { return Post::where('title', 'like', "%{$query}%") ->limit(10) ->get(); }};

sections.title: #Handling responses
sections.content:
JSON methods resolve with the return value on success, and reject on validation failure:
On success:
On validation failure:
Or using .catch():

examples.code: let data = await $wire.search('query')// data = [ { id: 1, title: '...' }, ...]
try { let data = await $wire.save()} catch (e) { // e.status = 422 // e.errors = { name: ['The name field is required.'] }}
$wire.save() .then(data => { // Handle success console.log(data) }) .catch(e => { if (e.status === 422) { // Handle validation errors console.log(e.errors) } })

sections.title: #Error rejection shape
sections.content:
When a promise is rejected, the error object has this structure:
For HTTP errors (500, etc.), the shape is the same but with the actual response data:

examples.code: { status: 422, // HTTP status code (422 for validation errors) body: null, // Raw response body (null for validation errors) json: null, // Parsed JSON (null for validation errors) errors: {...} // Validation errors object}
{ status: 500, body: '<html>...</html>', json: null, errors: null}

sections.title: #Behavior
sections.content:
The #[Json] attribute automatically applies two behaviors:
These behaviors match what you'd expect from an API-style endpoint.

sections.title: #When to use
sections.content:
Use #[Json] when:
Validation errors from JSON methods are only returned via promise rejection. They don't appear in $wire.$errors or the component's error bag. This is intentional—JSON methods are self-contained and don't affect the component's rendered state.
